explanation: The statement requests both an explanation of the planned path and a justification for why it is optimal. In LTL, we want to ensure that whenever the robot is planning a path (pathPlanning), the robot should eventually (at some point) provide both the explanation of the path (explainPath) and a justification for the decision (justifyDecision). Thus, we encode this as "Globally, if pathPlanning holds, then eventually both explainPath and justifyDecision must hold sometime in the future." This is captured as G(pathPlanning -> F(explainPath & justifyDecision)). This ensures that for every occurrence of pathPlanning, an explanation and justification will be given. The formula does not require them to be given immediately, but ensures they will eventually occur after any path planning. Prior possible encodings may have considered requiring explainPath and justifyDecision always together (G(explainPath & justifyDecision)), but that does not tie them to the pathPlanning activity and may be overly restrictive; this formula is the most faithful to the input's intent.

The property 'explainPath & justifyDecision -> pathPlanning' captures the natural language goal that the robot should explain the path it is planning to take ('explainPath') and why it is the optimal choice ('justifyDecision') when the robot is engaged in path planning ('pathPlanning'). To express that the robot should both explain its planned path and justify it whenever it is doing path planning, we can use the LTL formula: G (pathPlanning -> (explainPath & justifyDecision)). This ensures that globally, whenever path planning is being done by the robot, the robot must explain the path and provide a justification for its choice. This captures the intent from the natural language prompt accurately. Previous property suggestions (if there were any) might have failed if they did not enforce the explanation and justification specifically during path planning and instead disconnected these actions from the context of 'pathPlanning'.

To translate the natural language statement 'Explain path robot is planning to take and why it is the optimal choice,' we identify two tasks: (1) the robot should explain the path it plans to take, and (2) it should justify (explain why) the decision (why that path is optimal). This suggests that whenever the robot is planning a path (pathPlanning), it should (eventually) explain the path and justify why it chose it. In LTL, this is expressed by saying that globally, whenever 'pathPlanning' holds, both 'explainPath' and 'justifyDecision' will eventually happen. This can be captured as: G( pathPlanning -> (F explainPath & F justifyDecision) ). This formula ensures every instance of path planning is followed (at some future point) by an explanation and a justification, reflecting the requirement to both explain and justify the selected path. Previous translations might have neglected the timing requirement (that both explanation and justification should follow path planning), or might have omitted one of the components (explanation/justification). This formula accurately captures both aspects.

The input requires the robot to both explain the path it is planning to take and to justify why this path is the optimal choice, whenever it is planning a path. Thus, globally, whenever pathPlanning holds, it should also be the case that the robot will explain the path and justify its decision. These obligations can be represented by 'pathPlanning -> (explainPath & justifyDecision)', and we use the globally operator to indicate this holds at all times.

The natural language requirement asks the robot to both explain the planned path and justify why it is the optimal choice. In terms of temporal logic, this suggests that whenever the robot is engaged in path planning (pathPlanning), it should eventually explain the path (explainPath) and justify the decision (justifyDecision). This is captured by 'G (pathPlanning -> (F explainPath & F justifyDecision))', which means that globally, if path planning is happening, then both explaining the path and justifying the decision must eventually occur. This is the strongest direct encoding given the atomic propositions provided. Alternative LTL formulas that are less direct (e.g., ones that do not use both explainPath and justifyDecision) would not fulfill the natural language's demand for both explanation and justification.

The input natural language requests that the robot both explains the path it is planning to take and justifies why it is the optimal choice. The relevant atomic propositions are 'explainPath' (robot explains the planned path) and 'justifyDecision' (robot justifies its decision about the chosen path). The request implies that whenever the robot is planning a path ('pathPlanning'), it should ensure both an explanation and a justification occur. Therefore, the LTL property globally requires that whenever 'pathPlanning' holds, both 'explainPath' and 'justifyDecision' should also hold. This is captured by G(pathPlanning -> (explainPath & justifyDecision)).

The natural language input requests that the robot both explains the path it plans to take and justifies why that path is the optimal choice. The appropriate atomic propositions are 'explainPath' and 'justifyDecision'. In LTL, we want to specify that whenever the robot is planning its path (indicated by 'pathPlanning'), it should eventually provide both an explanation of the path (explainPath) and a justification for the decision (justifyDecision). This is expressed as: G(pathPlanning -> (F explainPath & F justifyDecision)), meaning that globally, whenever pathPlanning holds, eventually both explainPath and justifyDecision should hold in the future. This directly captures the requirement stated in the input. No previously given LTL formulas are present to compare.

The input natural language, 'Explain path robot is planning to take and why it is the optimal choice,' requires two main actions: the robot must (1) explain the path it is planning to take, and (2) justify why this path is the optimal choice. Translating this into LTL, we focus on ensuring that whenever the robot is engaged in path planning ('pathPlanning'), it will (eventually or immediately) provide both an explanation of the path ('explainPath') and a justification of the decision ('justifyDecision'). To capture this, we use the 'globally' (G) operator over an implication from 'pathPlanning' to 'eventually' ('F') 'explainPath' and 'eventually justifyDecision.' The resulting property is:

G(pathPlanning -> (F explainPath & F justifyDecision))

This guarantees that in any state where path planning is performed, there will in the future (not necessarily immediately) be an explanation and a justification, directly aligning with the requirement that the robot explains the path and why it is optimal. Previous possible translations that might only have required the explanation or justification, but not both, or did not relate them to path planning, would be incomplete. This property tightly links the required actions to the context ('pathPlanning'), matching the intent of the natural language statement.

The natural language input requests that the robot explain both the path it plans to take and why it is the optimal choice. Thus, the property must require: (1) that the robot explain the path it is planning ("explainPath"), and (2) that it justify the decision it made ("justifyDecision"). Interpreting this temporally, whenever the robot is engaged in path planning ("pathPlanning"), it should (eventually or globally within the same path planning episode) explain the chosen path and justify why it is optimal. The typical way to represent 'whenever path planning, eventually this happens' is: G(pathPlanning -> (F explainPath & F justifyDecision)). This LTL formula ensures that, during any period where path planning happens, both the path is explained and the choice is justified at some point after (or possibly at the same time). This best captures the requirements stated in the natural language. Previous properties might have omitted one of the two parts (explaining path or justification) or not connected them temporally with path planning activity.

The input requires that the robot both explains the path it is planning to take and justifies why the decision is optimal (i.e., explains the rationale for its choice) whenever the robot is engaged in path planning. This is best captured by stating that globally, whenever the robot is planning a path (pathPlanning holds), it is required that the robot will eventually explain the path (explainPath) and eventually justify the decision (justifyDecision). We use the globally operator (G) and implication to encode that this obligation holds at every moment pathPlanning is true. Both explainPath and justifyDecision must happen eventually after pathPlanning holds (can be at the same time or separately), so we use the conjunction of two eventualities: (F explainPath) & (F justifyDecision).
